questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin, metallic spherical shell carries a charge $Q$. A point charge $q$ is located at the centre of the shell and an additional charge $q_1$ is placed outside it, as shown in the figure. All three charges are positive. The force acting on the charge at the centre is
questions.solution:
The point charge $q$ at the centre of the metallic spherical shell induces a charge $-q$ on the inner surface of the shell.

Since the charge $q$ is placed exactly at the centre, the induced charge $-q$ is uniformly distributed over the inner surface. The electric field produced by this uniformly distributed charge at the centre is zero.

Due to the property of electrostatic shielding in conductors, the electric field inside the cavity of the conductor due to the external charge $q_1$ and the charge on the outer surface of the shell is zero.

Therefore, the net electric field at the centre of the shell is zero, and the force acting on the charge $q$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose a charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over a large plastic plate, producing an electric field of $10 \text{ V m}^{-1}$ at a point $P$ close to the centre of the plate. If this plastic plate is replaced by a copper plate having identical geometrical dimensions and carrying the identical charge $Q$, the electric field at point $P$ will be
questions.solution:
For a large non-conducting (plastic) plate with total charge $Q$ and surface area $A$, the electric field at a point close to its center can be found using Gauss's law. Taking a cylindrical Gaussian surface piercing through the plate, the total flux is $2EA = \dfrac{Q}{\epsilon_0} \Rightarrow E = \dfrac{Q}{2A\epsilon_0}$.

Given $E = 10 \text{ V m}^{-1}$.

When the plastic plate is replaced by a conducting (copper) plate of the same dimensions and carrying the same total charge $Q$, the charge distributes equally on its two outer surfaces. The charge on each surface is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$, and the surface charge density on each face is $\sigma' = \dfrac{Q}{2A}$.

The electric field at a point close to a conducting surface is given by $E' = \dfrac{\sigma'}{\epsilon_0}$.

Substituting $\sigma'$, we get $E' = \dfrac{Q}{2A\epsilon_0}$.

This expression is exactly the same as the electric field produced by the plastic plate. Thus, the electric field remains $10 \text{ V m}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a metallic particle with zero net charge is positioned close to a finite metal plate carrying a positive charge, the electric force acting on the particle will be
questions.solution:
When a neutral metallic particle is placed near a positively charged finite metal plate, electrostatic induction occurs.

The positive charge on the plate attracts the free electrons within the metallic particle. As a result, the side of the particle closer to the plate acquires a net negative induced charge, while the side farther away acquires an equal net positive induced charge.

Since the electric field produced by a finite charged plate decreases with distance, the attractive force exerted on the closer negative charge is greater in magnitude than the repulsive force exerted on the farther positive charge.

Therefore, the net electric force acting on the metallic particle is attractive, which means it is directed towards the plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a charge $q$ is positioned at the centre of the open end of a cylindrical vessel. The electric flux passing through the surface of the vessel is
questions.solution:
To find the electric flux passing through the surface of the cylindrical vessel, we can use Gauss's law.

Imagine placing an identical cylindrical vessel symmetrically over the open end of the given vessel to form a closed Gaussian surface (a complete cylinder).

The charge $q$ is now located exactly at the center of this closed cylindrical surface.

According to Gauss's law, the total electric flux passing through this entire closed surface is:

$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$

Due to the symmetry of the configuration, exactly half of the electric field lines will pass through the upper vessel and the other half will pass through the lower vessel.

Therefore, the electric flux passing through the surface of the given (lower) cylindrical vessel is:

$\Phi = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{total}}}{2} = \dfrac{q}{2\varepsilon_0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric charges occupy a small volume. The electric flux through a spherical surface of radius $10 \text{ cm}$ enclosing the entire charge is $25 \text{ V m}$. Determine the flux over a concentric sphere of radius $20 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, the total electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}$.

The flux depends only on the total charge enclosed by the surface and is independent of the size or shape of the closed surface.

Since the entire charge is enclosed by both the $10 \text{ cm}$ and $20 \text{ cm}$ spheres, the enclosed charge remains the same.

Therefore, the electric flux through the $20 \text{ cm}$ sphere will be the same as that through the $10 \text{ cm}$ sphere, which is $25 \text{ V m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin, metallic spherical shell contains a charge $Q$ on it. A point charge $q$ is placed at the centre of the shell and another charge $q_1$ is placed outside it as shown in the figure. All the three charges are positive. The force on the central charge due to the shell is
questions.solution:
According to the properties of conductors, the electric field inside the cavity of a conducting shell due to any external charges is always zero (electrostatic shielding).

The external charges in this case are the point charge $q_1$ and the charge distributed on the outer surface of the shell. Therefore, the electric field at the centre due to the outer surface charge ($\vec{E}_{\text{outer}}$) and the point charge $q_1$ ($\vec{E}_{q_1}$) must sum to zero:
$\vec{E}_{\text{outer}} + \vec{E}_{q_1} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{E}_{\text{outer}} = -\vec{E}_{q_1}$

The positive charge $q$ at the centre induces a charge $-q$ on the inner surface of the shell. Since the shell is spherical and $q$ is exactly at the centre, this induced charge is uniformly distributed. The electric field at the centre due to this uniformly distributed inner surface charge is zero ($\vec{E}_{\text{inner}} = 0$).

The total electric field at the centre due to the shell is the sum of the fields due to its inner and outer surfaces:
$\vec{E}_{\text{shell}} = \vec{E}_{\text{inner}} + \vec{E}_{\text{outer}} = 0 - \vec{E}_{q_1} = -\vec{E}_{q_1}$

Since $q_1$ is a positive charge located to the right of the shell, its electric field $\vec{E}_{q_1}$ at the centre is directed towards the left. Consequently, the electric field due to the shell $\vec{E}_{\text{shell}}$ is directed towards the right.

Since the central charge $q$ is positive, the force exerted on it by the shell is $\vec{F}_{\text{shell}} = q\vec{E}_{\text{shell}}$, which is directed towards the right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $q$ is situated at the centre of the open end of a cylindrical vessel, as shown in the figure. The electric flux through the surface of the vessel is
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, the total electric flux through a closed surface enclosing a charge $q$ is $\dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$.

The given cylindrical vessel is open at one end, and the charge $q$ is placed at the centre of this open end.

To apply Gauss's law, we can construct a symmetrical closed surface by imagining an identical inverted cylindrical vessel placed over the open end.

The charge $q$ is now enclosed at the centre of this combined closed cylindrical surface.

The total flux through this closed surface is $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$.

Due to symmetry, the flux through the original cylindrical vessel is exactly half of the total flux.

Therefore, the electric flux through the surface of the vessel is $\Phi = \dfrac{1}{2} \Phi_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{q}{2\varepsilon_0}$.
